articles.headline: Sinaloa Cartel Civil War Turns Culiacán Into Urban Battlefield
articles.subheadline: Eighteen months of factional fighting has driven a 70% surge in emergency calls as paramedics don body armor to respond
articles.body:
The Mexican city of Culiacán has descended into sustained urban warfare as rival factions of the Sinaloa cartel wage a bloody internal conflict that has persisted for a year and a half, leaving residents trapped between heavily armed combatants and a government security apparatus unable to stem the killing.

The violence traces back to the capture of Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada, one of the cartel's co-founders, who was lured to the United States in an elaborate sting operation facilitated by the son of another cartel leader. Zambada's removal fractured the organization — once described by locals as operating like a unified family — into warring camps now locked in a campaign of assassinations, kidnappings, and terror. The crisis in Sinaloa state carries a pointed warning following the death in custody on Sunday of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, known as "El Mencho," leader of the rival Jalisco New Generation cartel and Mexico's most wanted man. His removal could trigger similar power vacuums elsewhere.

In Culiacán, a prosperous northern city of shopping malls and car dealerships, the toll is staggering. Paramedic call-outs surged more than 70 percent last year, according to first responders who now wear 14 kilograms of Kevlar body armor on every shift. Local journalists report an average of five to six homicides per day, a rate that has held steady despite the deployment of thousands of Mexican troops and the establishment of military checkpoints across the city. Schools, hospitals, and funerals have all been targeted. Victims have included a 16-year-old boy shot dead while riding his bicycle, his body surrounded by more than a dozen bullet casings. Mutilated corpses bearing threatening messages from one faction to another have been left at shopping centers and along major roads.

The Mexican government has responded with a massive military presence, but residents and reporters who have covered the region for decades say the security forces have failed to reduce the killing. Members of one cartel faction, speaking to the BBC's Quentin Sommerville in a clandestine meeting while fully armed and masked, offered a chilling assessment: the war will continue until only one side remains, regardless of civilian casualties. Meanwhile, a group called Mothers Fighting Back, led by women whose children have disappeared, conducts searches of fields and roadsides for clandestine graves, probing the soil for the smell of human remains under military escort.

The crisis underscores the paradox at the heart of U.S.-Mexico counter-narcotics strategy. President Donald Trump has designated the Sinaloa cartel and others as terrorist organizations and labeled fentanyl a weapon of mass destruction, threatening direct military action if Mexico fails to contain the traffickers. Yet the very success of operations targeting cartel leadership — from El Mayo's capture to El Mencho's death — risks generating exactly the kind of destabilizing power struggles now consuming Culiacán, where the billion-dollar fentanyl enterprise that has devastated American communities has turned inward with devastating force.

articles.headline: Machine Learning Tackles Inverse Design of Exotic Metamaterials
articles.subheadline: Neural network models can now reverse-engineer lattice structures that shrink when heated and expand when compressed
articles.body:
Researchers have developed a machine learning framework capable of designing metamaterials that exhibit two counterintuitive properties simultaneously — shrinking when heated and expanding laterally when compressed — a breakthrough that could accelerate the deployment of these exotic structures in aerospace, precision instruments, and other demanding engineering applications.

Xiang-Long Peng and Bai-Xiang Xu, in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server on February 12, describe a suite of neural network models that solve the notoriously difficult inverse design problem for bimaterial thermoelastic lattice metamaterials. Rather than the traditional trial-and-error approach of guessing a structure's geometry and then simulating its behavior, the inverse models work backward: an engineer specifies the desired combination of negative Poisson's ratio and negative thermal expansion coefficient, and the system outputs the geometric features and base material pairings needed to achieve them.

The approach rests on a large dataset generated through computational homogenization — essentially high-throughput simulations of hybrid-honeycomb structures whose geometrical parameters and constituent material properties were systematically varied. The authors first trained a forward model that predicts effective thermoelastic properties from a given design, achieving what they describe as good predictive accuracy. They then built six distinct inverse models, each tailored to a different design scenario defined by varying combinations of target properties and adjustable structural variables. A key methodological innovation is the integration of the trained forward model directly into the loss functions of the inverse models, ensuring physical consistency during training and enabling the generation of supplementary datasets for cases where the base materials are held fixed.

Negative Poisson's ratio materials, known as auxetics, become thicker perpendicular to an applied stretch rather than thinner — a property useful for impact resistance and form-fitting structures. Negative thermal expansion, meanwhile, is critical in applications where dimensional stability under temperature swings is paramount, such as satellite components and optical systems. Achieving both properties simultaneously in a single structure has been a persistent materials design challenge, and brute-force computational searches across the vast parameter space are prohibitively expensive.

The machine learning models reported by Peng and Xu, who are affiliated with institutions working at the intersection of computational mechanics and materials science, reduce what would be days of simulation to near-instantaneous predictions. While the work remains at the computational stage — with no experimental fabrication reported — the framework is designed to be directly applicable to real engineering tasks, potentially lowering the barrier to deploying thermoelastic metamaterials in next-generation thermal management and structural systems.

articles.headline: Martian Ice Could Preserve Ancient Life for 50 Million Years, Study Finds
articles.subheadline: NASA and Penn State researchers say future Mars missions should drill into pure ice, not rock, to search for biosignatures
articles.body:
Ancient microbial life — or its chemical remnants — could survive tens of millions of years locked inside Martian ice, according to a new study from NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center and Penn State University that fundamentally reframes where future missions should look for signs of biology on the Red Planet.

Published in the journal Astrobiology, the research subjected E. coli bacteria sealed in pure water ice to gamma radiation equivalent to 50 million years of cosmic ray bombardment on the Martian surface, all while held at minus 60 degrees Fahrenheit to replicate conditions in Mars's polar regions. More than 10 percent of the amino acids — the molecular building blocks of proteins — remained intact after the full simulation. The result was unexpected: samples mixed with Mars-like silicate rock and clay degraded ten times faster, with organic material failing to survive the same exposure. A 2022 study by the same team had actually predicted the opposite, finding that a 10 percent ice and 90 percent soil mixture broke down organics more quickly than pure sediment alone, leading researchers to assume pure ice would fare even worse.

The key appears to be what happens at the boundary between ice and mineral grains. Lead author Alexander Pavlov, a space scientist at NASA Goddard, explained that when ice contacts mineral surfaces, a thin liquid film forms that allows radiation-generated reactive particles to move freely and attack organic compounds. In solid pure ice, those harmful particles become frozen in place, unable to reach and destroy the amino acids. The finding carries significant implications: because many of Mars's known surface ice deposits are less than two million years old — well within the 50-million-year preservation window — any biological material trapped inside should still be detectable.

The study also tested organic preservation at the far colder temperatures found on Jupiter's moon Europa and Saturn's moon Enceladus, where degradation slowed even further. That result bodes well for NASA's Europa Clipper mission, which launched in 2024 and is scheduled to reach Jupiter in 2030 to perform 49 close flybys of Europa's ice shell and probe for conditions that could support life beneath it.

For Mars exploration specifically, the research team argues that mission planners should prioritize drilling into clean, buried ice over analyzing rocks or soil. Co-author Christopher House, a Penn State geosciences professor, noted that while Mars harbors abundant ice, most of it sits just below the surface and will require robust drilling or scooping tools — capabilities demonstrated by NASA's 2008 Phoenix lander, which first excavated and photographed subsurface ice in Mars's arctic region. The study was funded through NASA's Planetary Science Division and involved researchers from both Penn State's Department of Geosciences and multiple divisions at Goddard Space Flight Center.

articles.headline: Trump Administration Directs Diplomats to Fight Foreign Data Laws
articles.subheadline: A State Department cable signed by Rubio frames data sovereignty regulations as threats to AI advancement and global commerce
articles.body:
The Trump administration has instructed U.S. diplomats worldwide to actively push back against foreign governments' data sovereignty laws, framing the growing wave of data localization mandates as an obstacle to artificial intelligence development and American technology leadership.

An internal diplomatic cable signed by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, first reported by Reuters on February 25, directs embassy staff to "counter unnecessarily burdensome regulations, such as data localization mandates" that require companies to store and process citizens' data within national borders. The cable argues that such laws "disrupt global data flows, increase costs and cybersecurity risks, limit AI and cloud services, and expand government control in ways that can undermine civil liberties and enable censorship." Diplomats have also been ordered to monitor legislative proposals that would promote data sovereignty frameworks and to advocate for the Global Cross-Border Privacy Rules Forum, an international body that facilitates cross-border data transfers through privacy certifications.

The directive arrives at a moment of intensifying global tension over how American technology giants and AI companies handle foreign citizens' personal information. The European Union has established the most comprehensive regulatory framework through the General Data Protection Regulation, the Digital Services Act, and the AI Act — a suite of laws designed to limit tech companies' exploitation of user data and impose accountability for algorithmic systems. Other nations across Asia, Latin America, and Africa have pursued or are considering similar data localization requirements.

The cable represents a significant escalation of the administration's posture on behalf of U.S. tech interests abroad, effectively enlisting the diplomatic corps as advocates for Silicon Valley's preferred regulatory environment. Critics of data sovereignty laws have long argued they fragment the internet and raise costs for businesses, while proponents contend they are essential to protecting citizens from surveillance and exploitation by foreign corporations.

The move aligns with the administration's broader strategy of removing regulatory barriers to American AI dominance, a priority that has also shaped domestic policy. The U.S. State Department did not respond to requests for comment on the cable. For major cloud and AI providers like Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and OpenAI — all of which depend on processing vast quantities of global data — the diplomatic offensive could prove consequential as governments weigh whether to impose stricter controls on cross-border data flows.

articles.headline: US Lends $26.5 Billion to Southern Co. for Power Projects
articles.subheadline: Trump administration closes massive financing package as data center boom strains grid capacity and affordability
articles.body:
The Trump administration finalized a $26.5 billion loan package for Southern Company power projects in Georgia and Alabama on Wednesday, marking one of the largest federal energy financing deals in recent memory as the nation grapples with surging electricity demand and growing concerns over affordability.

The loan, closed by the U.S. Department of Energy, is directed at bolstering power generation infrastructure operated by subsidiaries of Southern Company, one of the largest electric utilities in the United States. The deal comes at a moment when the American power grid faces unprecedented strain from the rapid proliferation of electricity-hungry data centers, driven largely by the artificial intelligence boom, as well as broader electrification trends across the economy.

Rising electricity costs have become a political flashpoint, with consumers and regulators alike raising alarms about the affordability of keeping the lights on. Data centers alone are projected to consume a rapidly growing share of U.S. electricity output in the coming years, and utilities across the Southeast — a region that has attracted significant investment from hyperscale cloud and AI operators — are scrambling to build out generation capacity to meet the demand. Southern Company, headquartered in Atlanta, serves approximately nine million customers across several southeastern states and has been at the center of these expansion efforts.

The scale of the federal financing underscores the administration's willingness to deploy substantial government capital to accelerate energy infrastructure buildout, even as debates continue over the appropriate mix of generation sources. Southern Company's portfolio includes nuclear, natural gas, and renewable assets, and the company has been a key player in the development of the Vogtle nuclear expansion in Georgia — a project that experienced significant cost overruns and delays before its final units came online.

The loan package is likely to face scrutiny from fiscal hawks and energy policy analysts who will examine whether the terms adequately protect taxpayers. But proponents argue that federal financing is essential to keeping pace with electricity demand that threatens to outstrip supply, potentially leading to reliability crises and further price increases for households and businesses across the region.

articles.headline: Blockfills CEO Steps Down as Crypto Lender Reels From $75M Loss
articles.subheadline: Chicago-based firm froze client withdrawals earlier this month and is now seeking a buyer amid broader market weakness
articles.body:
Nicholas Hammer, co-founder of institutional crypto lender Blockfills, has exited the chief executive role as the firm grapples with approximately $75 million in lending losses and a freeze on customer deposits and withdrawals that has persisted since February 11. Joseph Perry has been named interim CEO, according to the company's website.

The leadership shakeup at the Chicago-based firm, which processed more than $60 billion in trading volume in 2025 — a 28 percent increase year over year — underscores the fragility that still haunts crypto lending even as the industry matures. A person with direct knowledge of the situation told CoinDesk that some clients were encouraged to pull their assets from the platform before the withdrawal halt took effect, raising questions about whether all customers received equal treatment. Blockfills is now actively seeking a buyer, according to earlier reporting.

The episode carries uncomfortable echoes of the 2022 crypto winter, when Celsius, BlockFi, and Genesis each froze customer accounts as cascading losses tore through the sector. Blockfills serves roughly 2,000 institutional clients — hedge funds, asset managers, and mining operations among them — and counts Susquehanna Private Equity Investments, CME Ventures, and Nexo among its backers. The firm raised $37 million in a Series A round in January 2022.

Blockfills said in a February 11 statement that it was working with investors and clients to restore liquidity and that customers could still open and close positions in spot and derivatives markets. But as of publication, deposit and withdrawal capabilities remain suspended, leaving client funds in limbo. Hammer did not respond to requests for comment, and his LinkedIn profile still listed him as CEO as of February 25.

The turmoil arrives during a difficult stretch for digital assets more broadly. Bitcoin has remained below $70,000 following a sharp retreat from its late-2025 record high, while ether trades near $2,000, reflecting cautious sentiment across the market. The Blockfills situation will likely intensify calls for stronger risk management standards and regulatory oversight in institutional crypto lending — a segment that has repeatedly proved vulnerable to sudden market dislocations despite its growing scale.

articles.headline: Tether Pours $200M Into Digital Marketplace Whop at $1.6B Valuation
articles.subheadline: The stablecoin giant aims to embed USDT payments into a fast-growing creator economy platform with 18.4 million users
articles.body:
Tether, the company behind the $185 billion USDT stablecoin, is making its largest known push into consumer-facing commerce with a $200 million investment in Whop, an online marketplace for digital goods and creator communities. The deal values Whop at $1.6 billion, according to CEO Steven Schwartz.

Whop operates as a marketplace where creators sell access to software tools, trading groups, online communities, and educational courses. The platform claims 18.4 million users and roughly $3 billion in annual creator earnings, with gross transaction volume growing at approximately 25% month-over-month — a pace that, if sustained, signals explosive near-term scale. Under the terms of the investment, Whop will integrate Tether's crypto wallet infrastructure, enabling users and creators to hold, transact, and settle payments in USDT and Tether's newer U.S.-focused USAT token directly on the platform, bypassing traditional banks and card networks entirely.

The strategic logic for Tether is clear: embedding its stablecoins into the daily financial plumbing of a high-growth platform extends USDT's reach beyond trading desks and into routine online commerce. USDT is already widely used as a dollar-access tool in emerging markets, and the Whop integration could accelerate that trend. The funding will support Whop's geographic expansion into Latin America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific — regions where dollar-denominated stablecoins have found strong product-market fit due to currency volatility and limited banking infrastructure.

Whop also plans to use the capital to build lending and borrowing features powered by decentralized finance protocols, suggesting the platform could evolve from a simple marketplace into a broader financial services layer for the creator economy. For creators who currently depend on payment processors with multi-day settlement windows and cross-border fees, instant stablecoin settlement represents a meaningful upgrade.

The investment marks a notable shift in Tether's capital deployment strategy. While the company has historically focused on treasury management and reserve holdings — primarily U.S. Treasuries — the Whop deal signals a more aggressive venture posture aimed at embedding stablecoin infrastructure into platforms where millions of users already transact. If the integration succeeds, it could serve as a template for how stablecoin issuers compete not just on peg stability and reserves, but on distribution.

articles.headline: Jamie Dimon Warns Rivals Are Repeating Pre-2008 'Dumb Things' in Credit
articles.subheadline: JPMorgan CEO's concerns about reckless lending echo financial crisis parallels as macro analysts back his assessment
articles.body:
JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon has issued a pointed warning that competitors are engaging in reckless behavior in the credit markets, drawing explicit parallels to the kind of risk-taking that precipitated the 2008 global financial crisis.

Dimon, who has led the largest U.S. bank since 2005 and navigated it through the last major financial meltdown, said rivals are doing "dumb things" in credit — a characterization that Bloomberg macro strategist Simon White described as well founded. The comments come at a time when credit spreads remain historically tight and leveraged lending has surged, conditions that some analysts view as signs of complacency among market participants chasing yield in an increasingly competitive landscape.

The JPMorgan chief's warnings carry particular weight given his track record. During the lead-up to the 2008 crisis, Dimon was among the few major bank CEOs who recognized early signs of excess in mortgage-related credit products. His bank emerged from that episode in a stronger competitive position than most peers, in part because of a more disciplined approach to risk. Now, with private credit markets having ballooned past $1.7 trillion and traditional banks competing aggressively for lending business, Dimon appears to see a familiar pattern forming — one where the pressure to maintain market share leads institutions to underwrite deals with deteriorating terms and insufficient safeguards.

White's analysis supports the thesis that current credit market dynamics warrant concern. When spreads compress to levels that no longer adequately compensate for default risk, and when covenant protections erode in loan agreements, the system becomes more fragile and vulnerable to sudden repricing. The danger is not necessarily an imminent repeat of 2008, but rather that accumulated risk-taking could amplify the next economic downturn whenever it arrives.

For investors and regulators alike, Dimon's remarks serve as a reminder that periods of apparent market calm often mask the buildup of systemic vulnerabilities. Whether his peers heed the warning — or dismiss it as competitive posturing — may not be clear until the next credit cycle turns.

articles.headline: UBS Wealth CIO: Investors Should Pivot From Coders to Builders
articles.subheadline: AI disruption threatens software stocks; miners, power producers, and industrials stand to benefit, top strategist says
articles.body:
Investors should rotate out of software-heavy technology stocks and into companies that operate in the physical world — miners, power producers, and industrial firms — as artificial intelligence increasingly threatens to disrupt digital business models, according to a top UBS Wealth Management strategist.

Ulrike Hoffmann-Burchardi, global head of equities and chief investment officer for the Americas at UBS Wealth Management, argued that AI's rapid advance poses a material risk to software-backed businesses, many of which have commanded premium valuations for years on the assumption of durable competitive moats. As AI tools grow more capable of writing code, automating workflows, and replicating software functionality, those moats may prove far shallower than investors have priced in.

The recommendation marks a notable shift in tone from one of the world's largest wealth managers. For much of the past decade, the prevailing institutional view favored asset-light technology companies over capital-intensive industries. Hoffmann-Burchardi's thesis flips that calculus: the very physicality of mining, energy generation, and industrial manufacturing — the difficulty of replicating a copper mine or a power plant with a large language model — becomes a source of durable value rather than a drag on returns.

The argument also dovetails with broader market trends. Surging demand for data centers and AI infrastructure has already driven a re-rating of power utilities and energy companies, while industrial firms supplying the physical backbone of the AI buildout have seen renewed investor interest. At the same time, several high-flying software names have faced valuation compression as the market reassesses which businesses AI will augment and which it will commoditize.

For allocators, the message from UBS is straightforward: in an era where code can be generated at near-zero marginal cost, scarcity resides in atoms, not bits. Whether the market fully embraces that rotation remains to be seen, but the strategic case for real-world builders over digital incumbents is gaining influential advocates.

articles.headline: Crypto Super PAC That Pledged $100M for 2026 Midterms Has Zero Funds
articles.subheadline: Fellowship PAC announced with fanfare last September, but FEC filings show no money raised as primaries approach
articles.body:
A crypto-focused political action committee that drew widespread attention last September with a splashy promise of $100 million in campaign spending for the 2026 U.S. midterm elections has yet to raise a single dollar, according to Federal Election Commission filings.

The Fellowship PAC launched with a press release praising President Donald Trump's regulatory approach to digital assets and pledging to back "pro-innovation, pro-crypto candidates." Early reports linked stablecoin giant Tether as a potential backer, but the company this week distanced itself from the effort. "Tether International has no affiliation or oversight of Fellowship," spokesperson Alex Welch told CoinDesk, though she did not address whether Tether's newer U.S. operations had any connection. The PAC's original announcement identified no officers, donors, or key employees, and repeated attempts to contact the organization have gone unanswered.

The only visible thread connecting Fellowship to the broader crypto ecosystem is its FEC registration, which lists Mitchell Nobel as treasurer. Nobel serves as director of digital-assets strategy at Cantor Fitzgerald, the financial firm formerly led by Howard Lutnick — now Trump's Secretary of Commerce — and which has managed Tether's assets in recent years. Fellowship maintains a website and an account on X, where its most recent activity was reposting a comment from Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino earlier this month.

The PAC's dormancy raises questions about whether foreign-money restrictions may have complicated its plans. Under U.S. election law, political action committees cannot accept funding from non-U.S. entities. Any involvement from Tether — headquartered outside the United States — would have required that funds originate domestically and that political decisions not be guided by foreign nationals, a standard that has drawn heightened scrutiny during the Trump administration.

Meanwhile, the crypto industry's dominant political player, Fairshake, is already deploying resources. The super PAC — primarily funded by Coinbase, Andreessen Horowitz, and Ripple — has reported $193 million on hand and has begun targeting early 2026 races. In the 2024 election cycle, Fairshake and its affiliates backed more than 50 successful congressional candidates from both parties, demonstrating that nine-figure political spending can meaningfully shape crypto policy outcomes. With some of the earliest 2026 primaries fast approaching, any new entrant to the political spending arena risks arriving too late to matter.

articles.headline: Trump's Record-Length State of the Union Draws Sweeping Fact-Check Challenges
articles.subheadline: BBC Verify analysis finds multiple misleading or unsupported claims across inflation, gas prices, investment, and foreign policy
articles.body:
President Donald Trump's State of the Union address — at one hour and 47 minutes, the longest in decades — painted a triumphant picture of his administration's first year back in power, but independent fact-checkers found several of his marquee claims to be misleading, exaggerated, or lacking evidence.

On the economy, Trump told Congress that his policies are "rapidly ending" high prices created under his predecessor. While it is true that the annual inflation rate has eased to 2.4% as of January 2026, down from 3% during President Biden's final year, the picture is more complicated than Trump suggested. Beef prices, which he singled out as falling "significantly," actually rose 15% over the prior 12 months, with only a slight dip in the most recent month. Economists at Harvard University have estimated that Trump's own tariff regime added roughly 0.92 percentage points to consumer price inflation in January 2026. His claim that gasoline is "below $2.30 a gallon in most states" was particularly difficult to square with reality: the national average stood at $2.95 per gallon according to the AAA, with only Oklahoma averaging near $2.37 — the lowest in the country.

Trump's assertion that he had secured "more than $18 trillion" in investment commitments found no public corroboration. The White House's own investment tracker lists $9.7 trillion in pledges — a substantial figure, but barely half the president's claim. Greg Auclair of the Peterson Institute for International Economics cautioned that even tracked commitments "include pledges that may not materialise," pointing to a stalled EU trade deal as one example. Trump's statement that "more Americans are working today than at any time in history" is technically accurate — employment topped 158 million in January — but the employment-to-population ratio actually slipped from 60.1% to 59.8% since Biden left office, and unemployment edged up from 4.1% to 4.3%.

Foreign policy claims also drew scrutiny. Trump declared he had "ended eight wars" in his first ten months, but analysis showed that several of the conflicts listed by the White House lasted only days and one — a dispute between Egypt and Ethiopia over dam construction — involved no military combat at all. Fighting has resumed between Rwanda and the Democratic Republic of Congo despite a Washington-brokered agreement, and India publicly downplayed the U.S. role in resolving its brief clash with Pakistan. Trump's involvement in the Israel-Hamas ceasefire is his strongest claim to peacemaking credit.

On immigration, Trump's statement that "zero illegal aliens have been admitted" in nine months is accurate in the narrow sense that Border Patrol has not released any migrants from custody, according to the Department of Homeland Security. However, authorities still recorded 6,070 apprehensions at the southwest border in January 2026. His renewed assertion that electoral cheating is "rampant" remains unsupported: the Heritage Foundation's database documents just 1,620 proven fraud cases nationwide since 1982, and a Brennan Center study of 23.5 million votes in 2016 found only 30 suspected instances of non-citizen voting referred for investigation.

articles.headline: Diageo Shares Suffer Record Plunge as New CEO Signals Deep Turnaround
articles.subheadline: Incoming chief Dave Lewis cuts outlook, warning investors there is no quick fix for the British spirits giant
articles.body:
Diageo Plc suffered its largest single-day share decline on record on Tuesday after new Chief Executive Officer Dave Lewis delivered a sobering assessment of the challenges facing the world's biggest spirits maker, cutting the company's outlook and signaling that a meaningful recovery will take time.

The London-listed drinks giant, whose portfolio spans Johnnie Walker scotch, Guinness stout, and Tanqueray gin, saw its stock tumble as Lewis laid bare the depth of restructuring required. The sharp sell-off reflected investor dismay at the scale of problems accumulated at a company long considered one of Europe's most reliable consumer goods performers. Lewis, who built his reputation as a turnaround specialist during his tenure leading Tesco through its own crisis years ago, made clear that the path back to consistent growth would demand patience.

Diageo has been grappling with a confluence of headwinds in recent quarters, including softening demand in key markets, destocking by distributors, and shifting consumer preferences that have pressured volumes across several of its core spirits categories. The outlook cut suggests these pressures are proving more persistent than previously acknowledged by the company's leadership. Lewis's willingness to reset expectations early in his tenure follows a well-worn playbook for incoming CEOs — taking the pain upfront to establish a lower baseline from which future performance can be measured.

The record decline underscores just how far Diageo's standing has fallen among investors who once prized the stock for its defensive qualities and dependable dividend growth. The company's market capitalization has eroded significantly from its pandemic-era highs, when a boom in at-home drinking and premiumization trends propelled spirits sales to record levels. The subsequent normalization has been more painful than many analysts anticipated.

For Lewis, the immediate task will be convincing shareholders that Diageo's brand portfolio and global distribution network remain fundamentally strong assets, even as near-term financial performance disappoints. The market's reaction suggests that confidence is in short supply, and the new CEO faces a long road to rebuild it.

articles.headline: UK Commons Speaker Gave Police Flight Risk Tip Before Mandelson Arrest
articles.subheadline: Sir Lindsay Hoyle says he acted 'in good faith' passing information about the former ambassador's travel plans
articles.body:
Sir Lindsay Hoyle, the Speaker of the House of Commons, confirmed on Tuesday that he provided the Metropolitan Police with information suggesting Lord Peter Mandelson could be a flight risk — a tip that preceded the former Labour minister's arrest on Monday as part of a misconduct in public office investigation linked to Jeffrey Epstein.

Speaking before MPs after the Times first reported his involvement, Sir Lindsay said he passed on "relevant" information "in good faith" and considered it his "duty and responsibility" to do so. The BBC reported that Hoyle received intelligence during a visit to the British Virgin Islands last week indicating Mandelson was planning to travel there. Sir Lindsay expressed regret that the matter had quickly leaked to the media but offered no further details about the nature or source of his information.

The disclosure has injected a new layer of political intrigue into an already extraordinary case. Mandelson's legal team at Mishcon de Reya said their client had already agreed to a voluntary police interview next month, making the arrest unnecessary. They characterized the flight risk claim as "baseless" and have written to the Met demanding to know what evidence underpinned the decision to take him into custody. Adding to the confusion, Mandelson's lawyers said they were told twice by police that the referral came from Lord Forsyth, the Speaker of the House of Lords — a claim Forsyth has categorically denied as "entirely false and without foundation." The Met has declined to comment on the matter.

Mandelson was taken to Wandsworth police station in London on Monday afternoon and later released on bail, surrendering his passport as a condition. The investigation centers on allegations that, while serving as a government minister, he passed market-sensitive information to Epstein, the late convicted sex offender and financier. The allegations surfaced after the U.S. Department of Justice released a cache of documents last month, including emails between the two men. Mandelson, who was appointed British ambassador to the United States in February 2025, was dismissed from the post in September after Downing Street said new information had emerged about the depth of his relationship with Epstein.

Mandelson's legal team has maintained that he committed no criminal acts, was not motivated by financial gain, and intends to cooperate fully with investigators to "clear his name." The Metropolitan Police is understood to have conducted its own assessment of the credibility of the information Hoyle provided before proceeding with the arrest, though the force has offered no public explanation for its decision-making.

articles.headline: Physicists Uncover Fundamental Flaw in Wave Superposition for Layered Media
articles.subheadline: New research shows the superposition principle can fail for evanescent waves, then offers a fix that restores convergence
articles.body:
A pair of physicists has demonstrated that the superposition principle — one of the bedrock assumptions of linear wave physics — can break down when applied to multilayered media containing evanescent and inhomogeneous waves. The finding, published as a preprint on arXiv on February 17, challenges a foundational tool used across optics, acoustics, and electromagnetic theory.

Michael Mazilu and Andriejus Demčenko showed that when fields in layered systems are expanded as infinite series of evanescent wave components, the resulting superposition can diverge rather than converge to a physical solution. The problem emerges specifically in systems with three or more interfaces and arises because evanescent waves — fields that decay exponentially rather than propagate — cannot be properly normalized within the conventional mathematical basis. Crucially, the authors stress this is not a numerical artifact or computational error but a genuine mathematical failure of the standard representation.

The breakdown was identified using two well-established formalisms: the Airy formula, which describes multiple reflections within layered structures, and the scattering-matrix (S-matrix) approach widely used in photonics and condensed matter physics. In both frameworks, the researchers pinpointed the conditions under which partial-wave expansions involving evanescent modes produce divergent series, rendering standard analytical techniques unreliable.

To remedy the problem, Mazilu and Demčenko introduce what they call "power flux modes" — an orthonormal basis of wave solutions constructed to preserve energy conservation during scattering events. They prove that in this flux-orthonormal basis, scattering at each interface becomes unitary and propagation eigenvalues remain bounded, which together guarantee that series expansions converge. The approach eliminates the need for ad hoc regularization or renormalization procedures that physicists and engineers have sometimes employed when encountering divergent evanescent-mode sums.

The framework applies broadly across scalar, electromagnetic, and elastic wave systems, suggesting potential implications for fields ranging from thin-film optics and semiconductor device modeling to seismology and ultrasonic nondestructive testing. Multilayered structures are ubiquitous in modern technology — from anti-reflection coatings and photonic crystals to acoustic metamaterials — making a rigorous treatment of evanescent mode convergence practically significant.

While the paper is currently posted in the General Physics section of arXiv and has not yet undergone formal peer review, it addresses a subtle but important gap in the mathematical foundations of wave physics that has persisted largely unexamined. If validated, the power flux mode framework could become a standard tool for any analysis involving evanescent fields in stratified systems.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Surges Past $67,500 as Massive Short Squeeze Unwinds
articles.subheadline: Over $307 million in bearish bets liquidated in 24 hours as crypto market snaps weeks of extreme fear
articles.body:
Bitcoin rallied more than 5% to approximately $67,500 on Wednesday, catalyzing a broad relief rally across digital assets after weeks of deeply negative sentiment and crowded short positions that left the market primed for a sharp reversal.

The move liquidated over $307 million in leveraged bearish bets across crypto derivatives in just 24 hours, according to CoinGlass data. Major altcoins outpaced bitcoin's gains, with ether climbing roughly 10% to reclaim the $2,000 level for the first time in a week, while solana, dogecoin, cardano, and chainlink each posted advances exceeding 10%. The rally came after the widely watched Crypto Fear & Greed Index spent most of February mired in "Extreme Fear" territory, and perpetual futures funding rates had repeatedly turned negative — a telltale sign that bearish positioning had become overcrowded.

Crypto-related equities followed suit. Stablecoin issuer Circle surged 20% after beating earnings estimates, while Coinbase, Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy), and Galaxy Digital each gained between 5% and 6%. Bitcoin mining stocks including Bitfarms, Bitdeer, and MARA Holdings extended their own rebounds. Markus Thielen of 10x Research noted that many crypto-linked stocks had accumulated sizable short interest from hedge funds, setting the stage for the sharp upside reversal.

Perhaps more significant than the price action itself were signs that U.S. institutional buyers may be re-entering the market. The Coinbase Premium Index — which tracks the price gap between bitcoin on Coinbase and the global average and serves as a proxy for American capital flows — turned positive for the first time in over 40 days. Meanwhile, U.S. spot bitcoin ETFs recorded $257.7 million in inflows on Tuesday, the largest single-day total since early February. Notably, bitcoin perpetual funding rates remained below neutral even during the rally, suggesting the bounce was not driven by aggressive new leveraged speculation.

The crypto rebound unfolded against a supportive backdrop in traditional markets, with the S&P 500 up 0.6% and the Nasdaq 100 gaining 1.1% in early trading. Still, bitcoin remains down roughly 25% on the year, and whether the current move represents a durable trend change or merely a mechanical unwinding of extreme bearish positioning remains an open question. The speed and breadth of the rally, however, suggest that the pain trade has decisively shifted against the shorts — at least for now.

articles.headline: Wall Street Rallies Ahead of Nvidia Earnings as AI Anxiety Mounts
articles.subheadline: Investors push stocks higher seeking reassurance that the artificial intelligence boom remains on track
articles.body:
U.S. equities climbed on Monday as traders positioned ahead of Nvidia Corp.'s closely watched earnings report, with markets looking to the chipmaker for signals that demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure has not begun to falter.

The pre-earnings rally reflected a market caught between two competing impulses: lingering optimism about AI's transformative potential and deepening unease about whether the massive capital expenditures flowing into the technology will ultimately deliver returns. Nvidia, whose graphics processors have become the backbone of AI training and inference workloads, has emerged as the single most important barometer of the sector's health. Its quarterly results have repeatedly moved hundreds of billions of dollars in market capitalization in a single session, making each earnings release a de facto referendum on the AI trade.

The stakes this quarter are particularly elevated. Concerns about the sustainability of AI spending have intensified in recent months, fueled by questions about whether hyperscale cloud providers can maintain their breakneck pace of data center investment. Any sign of softening demand or margin pressure from Nvidia could ripple across the broader technology sector and weigh on major indices where mega-cap tech stocks carry outsized influence.

Traders appear to be betting that Nvidia will deliver another round of strong results, potentially calming nerves across a market that has grown increasingly sensitive to AI-related headlines. The company's guidance for coming quarters will likely draw even more scrutiny than its backward-looking numbers, as investors try to gauge whether the current wave of infrastructure buildout has legs or is approaching a plateau.

The broader rally underscored how central the AI narrative has become to equity market sentiment. With Nvidia serving as a proxy for tens of trillions of dollars in expected economic value creation from artificial intelligence, the company's earnings call has taken on significance that extends well beyond a single stock report — effectively setting the tone for risk appetite across global markets.

articles.headline: US to Raise Tariffs to 15% 'Where Appropriate,' Trade Rep Greer Says
articles.subheadline: Trump set to sign supplemental proclamation as administration signals continuity in trade policy
articles.body:
U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced on Tuesday that President Donald Trump intends to sign a supplemental proclamation raising tariff rates to 15 percent in cases deemed appropriate, signaling an escalation in the administration's trade agenda.

Greer framed the move as part of the administration's broader push for "continuity" in its tariff strategy, suggesting the increase is designed to maintain pressure on trading partners and reinforce existing trade measures rather than mark a dramatic shift in policy direction. The supplemental proclamation would give the president authority to selectively raise duties on targeted imports, though Greer did not specify which goods or countries would face the higher rate.

The announcement comes as the Trump administration continues to wield tariffs as a central instrument of economic and foreign policy. Since returning to office, Trump has pursued an aggressive trade posture, imposing and threatening levies across a range of sectors and trading partners. A move to 15 percent on select imports would represent a meaningful step up from some existing rates and could affect supply chains, consumer prices, and diplomatic relationships depending on the scope of the proclamation.

Market participants and trade analysts will be closely watching for details on which product categories and countries fall under the "where appropriate" designation. The language suggests the administration is preserving flexibility to apply the higher tariff selectively, potentially as leverage in ongoing or future trade negotiations. Industries reliant on imported materials and components could face increased costs, which historically have been passed along to consumers or absorbed through thinning margins.

The timing and specifics of the supplemental proclamation have not yet been disclosed. Trading partners are likely to respond with their own assessments of the measure, and retaliatory actions remain a possibility depending on the breadth of the tariff increase.

articles.headline: Chemists Synthesize 'Impossible' Silicon Aromatic Molecule After 50 Years
articles.subheadline: Pentasilacyclopentadienide, a five-atom silicon ring with full aromatic stability, could reshape catalysis and materials science
articles.body:
A team at Saarland University in Germany has created a silicon-based aromatic molecule that chemists worldwide spent nearly five decades trying — and failing — to produce. The compound, pentasilacyclopentadienide, replaces all five carbon atoms in a classic aromatic ring with silicon, achieving a level of stability long considered theoretically possible but practically out of reach. The results were published in the journal Science on February 24.

David Scheschkewitz, professor of general and inorganic chemistry at Saarland University, led the effort alongside doctoral student Ankur and crystallographer Bernd Morgenstern. Their synthesis marks only the second known class of silicon-based aromatic compound. The first — a three-membered silicon ring analogous to cyclopropenium — was produced in 1981. Every subsequent attempt to build larger all-silicon aromatic systems had ended in failure, making the five-membered ring a kind of white whale for inorganic chemists.

Aromaticity is one of the most consequential phenomena in chemistry. Aromatic molecules feature electrons shared evenly across a flat ring structure, obeying a quantum mechanical counting rule formulated by physicist Erich Hückel. That delocalization grants the molecules exceptional thermodynamic stability, which is why aromatic carbon compounds already underpin vast swaths of industrial chemistry — from plastics catalysis in polyethylene and polypropylene production to pharmaceutical synthesis. Achieving the same aromatic stability with silicon is significant because the element behaves differently from carbon at a fundamental level: it is more metallic and holds its electrons less tightly, properties that could yield catalysts and materials with entirely novel characteristics.

In a notable coincidence, a group led by Takeaki Iwamoto at Tohoku University in Sendai, Japan, independently synthesized the same compound at almost the same time. The two teams agreed to publish their findings side by side in the same issue of Science, lending additional confidence to the result and underscoring the compound's importance to the field.

The practical implications remain to be explored, but researchers say the breakthrough opens a new frontier. Silicon aromatic compounds could serve as ligands for catalysts with tunable electronic properties, or as building blocks for advanced materials that have no carbon-based analogue. After decades in which the molecule existed only as a theoretical curiosity, its physical realization gives chemists a concrete platform from which to investigate an unexplored region of the periodic table's chemistry.

articles.headline: Senate Probe Targets Binance Over Alleged $1.7B in Iran-Linked Transfers
articles.subheadline: Sen. Blumenthal demands records on suspicious flows to sanctioned entities and the firing of internal investigators who flagged them
articles.body:
Senator Richard Blumenthal, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Homeland Security Committee, has launched a formal inquiry into Binance over allegations that $1.7 billion in cryptocurrency was routed through the exchange to organizations linked to Iran, including Yemen's Houthi militants.

In a letter sent Tuesday to Binance co-chief executive Richard Teng, Blumenthal requested records pertaining to the exchange's dealings with two Hong Kong-based entities identified as the origin of the suspect transfers. One of the accounts was reportedly registered to Blessed Trust, a Hong Kong firm that operated as a Binance vendor. The senator also demanded documentation on the suspension and dismissal of compliance staff who initially uncovered the alleged violations — a detail that has drawn scrutiny from multiple news outlets including the New York Times, Wall Street Journal, and Fortune. "Binance appears to have ignored warnings and recommendations to prevent Iranian money laundering schemes on its cryptocurrency exchange," Blumenthal wrote.

Binance has firmly denied the allegations, calling prior reporting inaccurate and insisting it maintains strict know-your-customer and compliance protocols. "There are no Iranian users on the platform," a company spokesperson said, adding that claims about retaliatory firings of investigators are false. The exchange said it canceled the flagged accounts and severed ties with Blessed Trust in January. In a blog post over the weekend, Binance characterized its "sanctions-related exposure" as minimal and disclosed that an ongoing internal review would be submitted to the U.S. Justice Department by February 25.

The probe reopens a fraught chapter in Binance's regulatory history. In November 2023, founder and former CEO Changpeng Zhao pleaded guilty to violating anti-money-laundering laws and facilitating transactions with customers in sanctioned nations, including Iran. Binance agreed to pay $4.3 billion in penalties and exit the U.S. market as part of the settlement. Zhao served four months in prison before receiving a pardon from President Donald Trump.

The Senate investigation adds a new layer of political and legal risk for the world's largest crypto exchange at a time when the broader digital asset market remains under pressure. If the allegations are substantiated, Binance could face additional enforcement actions that would test the durability of its 2023 settlement with U.S. authorities and raise fresh questions about the effectiveness of compliance reforms implemented under its current leadership.

articles.headline: Nvidia Earnings Arrive at Pivotal Moment for AI-Driven Market
articles.subheadline: Investors brace for Wednesday's results as growing anxiety over AI's trajectory weighs on sentiment
articles.body:
Nvidia Corp. is set to report quarterly earnings on Wednesday afternoon, delivering what may be the most consequential single-company disclosure for U.S. equity markets this quarter. The chipmaker's results land at a moment when investor confidence in the artificial intelligence trade — the dominant force propelling stocks over the past two years — is showing visible signs of strain.

The stakes extend well beyond Nvidia's own $3 trillion-plus market capitalization. As the primary supplier of the advanced GPUs powering AI training and inference workloads, Nvidia has become a barometer for the entire AI investment cycle. Its revenue guidance and commentary on demand trends from hyperscale cloud customers will be parsed for signals about whether the hundreds of billions of dollars flowing into AI infrastructure are translating into sustainable returns — or whether the market has gotten ahead of itself.

Nervousness has been building across the technology sector in recent weeks. Questions about the pace of AI monetization, the emergence of more efficient open-source models, and the sheer scale of capital expenditure commitments from companies like Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, and Alphabet have left investors searching for reassurance. Nvidia's report will either provide that reassurance or amplify existing doubts at a time when the market has demonstrated little tolerance for disappointment.

The broader market's sensitivity to Nvidia's performance is itself a risk factor that analysts have flagged with increasing frequency. A narrow leadership cohort — heavily concentrated in AI-exposed mega-cap names — has driven a disproportionate share of index-level gains. Any stumble from Nvidia could reverberate through the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, potentially triggering a broader reassessment of valuations that have been premised on aggressive AI growth assumptions.

Wall Street consensus expectations heading into the report remain elevated, reflecting Nvidia's extraordinary recent track record of beating forecasts. But the margin for error has narrowed considerably. Investors are no longer willing to simply reward revenue beats; they want clarity on forward demand visibility, supply chain dynamics for next-generation Blackwell chips, and evidence that AI spending is accelerating rather than plateauing. Wednesday's after-hours session promises to be one of the most closely watched market events of 2026.

articles.headline: Engineered Bacteria Designed to Devour Tumors From the Inside Out
articles.subheadline: University of Waterloo team programs soil microbes with DNA 'circuits' to colonize and consume solid cancers
articles.body:
Researchers at the University of Waterloo have engineered bacteria capable of infiltrating the oxygen-starved cores of solid tumors and consuming them from within, a synthetic biology approach that could open an entirely new front in cancer treatment.

The strategy centers on Clostridium sporogenes, a common soil bacterium that thrives exclusively in oxygen-free environments. Because the interior of many solid tumors consists of dead cells with virtually no oxygen, these microbes find an ideal habitat once introduced as spores. "Bacteria spores enter the tumor, finding an environment where there are lots of nutrients and no oxygen, which this organism prefers, and so it starts eating those nutrients and growing in size," said Marc Aucoin, a chemical engineering professor at Waterloo. The bacterium effectively colonizes the tumor's core and begins dismantling it.

The central engineering challenge has been what happens at the tumor's edges, where traces of oxygen persist. As the bacteria expand outward from the core, they encounter enough oxygen to kill them off before they can eliminate the full mass of cancerous tissue. To overcome this, the Waterloo team inserted a gene from a related, more oxygen-tolerant bacterium — but they needed a safety mechanism to prevent it from activating prematurely. Bacteria circulating in the oxygen-rich bloodstream with that gene switched on could pose serious risks to patients. The solution was quorum sensing, a natural bacterial communication system in which microbes release chemical signals that intensify as their population grows. Only after a critical mass of bacteria has accumulated inside a tumor does the signal reach the threshold needed to activate the oxygen-resistance gene. The result is a tightly controlled biological trigger.

In work published in ACS Synthetic Biology, the team demonstrated this quorum-sensing circuit by programming bacteria to produce a green fluorescent protein at the intended activation point, confirming the system's reliability. "Using synthetic biology, we built something like an electrical circuit, but instead of wires we used pieces of DNA," said Brian Ingalls, a professor of applied mathematics at Waterloo. "Each piece has its job. When assembled correctly, they form a system that works in a predictable way." The next milestone is combining the oxygen-tolerance gene and the quorum-sensing control mechanism into a single organism and testing it against tumors in pre-clinical trials.

The research grew out of doctoral work by Bahram Zargar, now co-founder of Toronto-based CREM Co Labs, and was significantly advanced by former Waterloo PhD student Sara Sadr. The project reflects a broader push in oncology toward programmable biological therapeutics — treatments that can be designed with logic gates and feedback loops, much like software, but built from living components. If pre-clinical results hold up, engineered bacterial therapies could eventually complement surgery, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy as a tool against the solid tumors that account for roughly 90 percent of all cancers.

articles.headline: MatX Raises $500M to Build AI Chips Aimed at Dethroning Nvidia
articles.subheadline: The startup, founded by two ex-Google TPU engineers, plans to fabricate chips with TSMC and begin shipping in 2027
articles.body:
MatX, an AI chip startup founded by former Google hardware engineers, has closed a $500 million Series B round as it races to develop processors it claims will be ten times more efficient than Nvidia's GPUs at training large language models.

The round was co-led by trading firm Jane Street and Situational Awareness, the investment fund launched by Leopold Aschenbrenner, a former OpenAI researcher who has become one of the most prominent voices arguing that artificial general intelligence is imminent. Additional participants include Marvell Technology, NFDG, Spark Capital, and Stripe co-founders Patrick and John Collison. MatX CEO Reiner Pope disclosed the funding Tuesday in a LinkedIn post, though the company did not reveal its latest valuation. For context, rival chip startup Etched reportedly raised its own $500 million round last month at a $5 billion valuation, according to Bloomberg.

The new capital represents a dramatic step up for MatX, which raised approximately $100 million in its Series A in 2024 at a valuation exceeding $300 million. The company was founded in 2023 by Pope, who previously led AI software development for Google's custom TPU chips, and Mike Gunter, a former lead hardware designer on the same TPU program. Their combined experience architecting one of the few chip platforms that has proven competitive with Nvidia's dominance gives MatX a credibility edge in an increasingly crowded field of GPU challengers.

The funding will be directed toward fabricating MatX's chips at TSMC, the world's leading semiconductor foundry, with initial shipments targeted for 2027. That timeline puts the startup on a collision course not only with Nvidia's next-generation GPU roadmap but also with a growing roster of competitors — including Etched, Cerebras, and Groq — all vying to carve out share in the booming AI accelerator market.

The investment also reflects a broader trend of financial heavyweights outside traditional venture capital pouring into AI infrastructure bets. Jane Street, best known as a quantitative trading powerhouse, and Aschenbrenner's fund both signal that sophisticated capital allocators see the AI chip supply chain as a strategic bottleneck worth billions in future returns. Whether MatX can translate its Google TPU pedigree and ambitious performance claims into silicon that ships on time will be the defining test of its next chapter.

articles.headline: Digital Twin Plus Bayesian Optimization Speeds Accelerator Tuning by 4-6x
articles.subheadline: ISAC post-accelerator team demonstrates hybrid approach that decouples optics from steering, achieving mid- to high-90% beam transmission
articles.body:
A team of physicists working on the ISAC post-accelerator has developed a hybrid tuning strategy that combines digital twin modeling with Bayesian optimization, reducing the iterations needed to achieve high-transmission beam configurations by a factor of four to six compared to conventional approaches.

The method, detailed in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server on February 23, works by factorizing the tuning problem into two distinct stages. First, a computational model — functioning as a digital twin of the accelerator — pre-sets the machine's optical elements by computing end-to-end tunes in a matter of seconds. Then, with optics already configured, Bayesian optimization handles the remaining beam steering under live operational conditions. The key insight is that decoupling these two tasks dramatically shrinks the parameter space that the optimization algorithm must search, leading to faster and more predictable convergence.

The research team — O. Hassan, O. Shelbaya, P. M. Jung, O. Kester, T. Planche, and W. Fedorko — validated their approach through three experimental case studies conducted with actual beam. In each case, the hybrid method outperformed a fully Bayesian baseline that attempted to optimize both optics and steering simultaneously under identical conditions. Final average beam transmissions consistently reached the mid- to high-90 percent range, a notable achievement for post-accelerator operations where losses can be difficult to control.

Beyond raw performance, the researchers emphasize practical advantages for day-to-day accelerator operations. The digital twin interfaces directly with the control system while respecting device bounds, slew-rate limits, and loss interlocks — critical safety constraints that can complicate purely algorithmic tuning approaches. By handling optics through the model rather than through online optimization, operational safeguards become easier to enforce, reducing the risk of beam loss or equipment damage during the tuning process.

The work reflects a broader trend in accelerator physics toward leveraging machine learning and computational modeling to automate and accelerate facility operations. As particle accelerators grow more complex and beam time becomes increasingly precious, methods that can reliably configure machines in fewer iterations offer significant savings in both time and resources. The ISAC facility, located at TRIUMF in Canada, serves a diverse user community studying nuclear structure, astrophysics, and materials science, making efficient beam delivery a persistent operational priority.

articles.headline: US Trade Rep Greer Signals 15% Tariff Increase 'Where Appropriate'
articles.subheadline: Trump administration pursues tariff escalation while pressing EU, UK, and USMCA partners on trade compliance
articles.body:
U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced Tuesday that President Donald Trump intends to sign a supplemental proclamation raising tariff levels to 15 percent in targeted areas, signaling a renewed escalation in the administration's trade agenda as it seeks what Greer described as "continuity" in its tariff strategy.

Speaking in an interview with Bloomberg, Greer indicated the increase would apply "where appropriate," though he did not specify which sectors or trading partners would face the higher duties. The move suggests the White House is methodically expanding its tariff framework rather than pursuing one-off actions, building on an approach that has already rattled global supply chains and drawn sharp responses from major trading partners.

Greer also turned his attention to existing trade agreements, saying the administration expects both the United Kingdom and the European Union to fully honor their current trade commitments with the United States. While the U.S. does not have a comprehensive free trade agreement with the EU, the two sides maintain a complex web of trade arrangements and ongoing negotiations that the Trump administration appears eager to leverage.

Perhaps most consequentially for North American commerce, Greer flagged the administration's intent to address what it views as gaps in the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, the trilateral deal that replaced NAFTA during Trump's first term. The USMCA, which took effect in July 2020, includes a built-in review mechanism, but the administration's desire to "fix" perceived shortcomings signals that Canada and Mexico could face fresh pressure to renegotiate key provisions — potentially affecting sectors from automotive manufacturing to agriculture and digital trade.

The combination of a new tariff proclamation and demands for tighter enforcement of existing deals paints a picture of an administration pursuing trade leverage on multiple fronts simultaneously. For businesses operating across borders, the announcement adds another layer of uncertainty to an already volatile trade environment, with the 15 percent figure likely to become a focal point for industries assessing their exposure to further escalation.

articles.headline: Man Sentenced to Life for Murder of Nine-Year-Old Girl in Random Street Attack
articles.subheadline: Deividas Skebas, 26, stabbed Lilia Valutyte as she played with a hula hoop outside her mother's shop in Boston, Lincolnshire
articles.body:
A 26-year-old man has been sentenced to life in prison with a minimum term of 25 years for the murder of Lilia Valutyte, a nine-year-old girl who was stabbed to death in a random daytime attack while playing outside her mother's workplace in Boston, Lincolnshire, in July 2022.

Deividas Skebas, a Lithuanian national formerly residing in Boston, was convicted by a jury at Lincoln Crown Court following an eight-day trial. He had admitted to killing Lilia but denied murder, arguing his responsibility was diminished by his mental state. Three psychiatrists confirmed Skebas had schizophrenia and was exhibiting psychotic symptoms on the day of the attack, and he told police after his arrest that he was being controlled by a microchip. The jury nonetheless found him guilty of murder, rejecting the diminished responsibility defense.

Mr. Justice Akhlaq Choudhury, delivering the sentence, described the killing as a "shocking and horrific act of violence." He told Skebas, who appeared via video link from Rampton Hospital in Nottinghamshire and showed little emotion: "You headed straight towards her, pulled out the knife, and as soon as you approached the unsuspecting Lilia, you plunged the knife into her heart." The judge noted that CCTV footage from moments before the attack showed a "carefree, happy girl" who "should have been safe." Skebas will remain at the psychiatric facility but will be transferred to prison to serve the remainder of his sentence if he is ever deemed well enough for discharge.

The attack occurred on July 28, 2022, on Fountain Lane in Boston, where Lilia had been playing with a hula hoop near the shop where her mother, Lina Savicke, worked. Skebas ran toward the child and stabbed her once in the chest. Savicke told the court that Lilia called out her name before collapsing into her arms. Despite emergency intervention from an off-duty police officer, doctors, and paramedics, the girl died within an hour from a single stab wound to her heart.

In a statement issued after sentencing, Lilia's family said the decision would not change their lives. "Nothing will bring our child back, the pain will not disappear, and the tears shed will not wash it away," the statement read. They expressed deep gratitude to those who supported them, adding: "No matter how strong we may appear, there are moments when all we need is to know that we are needed and not alone."

articles.headline: Wayve Secures $1.2B to Scale Its AI-First Autonomous Driving Platform
articles.subheadline: The UK startup, now valued at $8.6 billion, draws backing from Nvidia, Uber, Mercedes-Benz, Nissan, and Stellantis
articles.body:
British autonomous driving startup Wayve has closed a $1.2 billion funding round backed by an unusual coalition of tech giants, automakers, and institutional investors, signaling intensifying commercial interest in self-driving technology that can be sold as a platform rather than operated as a service.

The round, which could swell to $1.5 billion with an additional $300 million from Uber tied to robotaxi deployment starting in London, values Wayve at $8.6 billion. Eclipse, Balderton, and SoftBank Vision Fund 2 led the investment, with participation from Mercedes-Benz, Nissan, Stellantis, and returning backers Microsoft, Nvidia, and Uber. New institutional investors include Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan, Baillie Gifford, and British Business Bank.

What distinguishes Wayve from better-known rivals like Waymo and Tesla is its business model. Founded in 2017 by CEO Alex Kendall, the company has no ambition to operate robotaxi fleets or build its own vehicles. Instead, it sells its end-to-end deep learning software — what it calls "embodied AI" — directly to automakers and mobility platforms. The system requires no high-definition maps, works with whatever sensors and chips an automaker already has in its vehicles, and learns to drive entirely from data. Kendall told TechCrunch that this hardware-agnostic approach is what makes the licensing model viable: "If you build an autonomy stack that's specific to a sensor or compute architecture, you can't take option three."

The strategy is already generating commercial traction. Nissan plans to integrate Wayve's software into its advanced driver-assistance systems beginning in 2027, while Uber intends to launch commercial trials with Wayve-equipped vehicles later this year. Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi said the two companies plan to deploy together in more than 10 markets worldwide, working across multiple vehicle manufacturers. Wayve's latest Gen 3 platform, built on Nvidia's Drive AGX Thor computing architecture, is designed to support both hands-free assisted driving and fully driverless Level 4 operation on city streets and highways.

The size and composition of the round reflect a broader industry conviction that autonomous driving is entering its commercialization phase. Nvidia, which has maintained a close development relationship with Wayve since 2018 and had previously signaled a potential $500 million strategic investment, participated again, though Kendall declined to specify the chipmaker's contribution. For automakers like Mercedes-Benz and Stellantis, backing Wayve represents a bet that licensing third-party AI may prove faster and cheaper than developing proprietary self-driving systems — a calculus that could reshape competitive dynamics across the automotive industry as the technology matures.

articles.headline: Stripe Explores Potential Acquisition of PayPal in Landmark Deal
articles.subheadline: The $159 billion payments giant is in early-stage discussions to acquire some or all of PayPal, valued at roughly $40 billion.
articles.body:
Stripe, the Dublin-headquartered payments infrastructure company, is exploring a deal to acquire some or all of PayPal Holdings, according to a Bloomberg report citing people familiar with the matter. If consummated, the transaction would represent one of the largest deals in fintech history, combining two of the most prominent names in digital payments.

The discussions remain in their earliest stages, and there is no guarantee a deal will materialize. Still, the mere prospect sent PayPal's stock ticking upward on Monday. PayPal, which trades publicly and owns the Venmo peer-to-peer payments app alongside its flagship platform, currently carries a market capitalization of approximately $40 billion — a fraction of what it commanded at its pandemic-era peak. Stripe declined to comment on the report.

The acquisition interest emerged on the same day Stripe announced a tender offer valuing the privately held company at $159 billion, a 74 percent jump from its previous valuation. The new round of share purchases includes participation from Andreessen Horowitz and Thrive Capital, with Stripe also buying back some employee stock. The valuation cements Stripe's position as one of the most valuable private companies in the world. Co-founder and CEO Patrick Collison recently told CNBC that taking the company public is not a near-term priority.

A combination of Stripe and PayPal would unite two fundamentally different but complementary business models. Stripe has built its dominance as the behind-the-scenes payments infrastructure for internet businesses, powering checkout and financial services for millions of companies. PayPal, by contrast, operates a consumer-facing digital wallet and marketplace payments ecosystem that processes hundreds of billions of dollars in annual volume. Together, the combined entity would span virtually the entire digital payments value chain, from developer APIs to consumer wallets.

The potential deal also underscores the divergent trajectories of the two companies. While Stripe's valuation has surged on the strength of consistent revenue growth and expanding enterprise adoption, PayPal has struggled to recapture investor confidence after years of slowing growth and intensifying competition from Apple Pay, Block, and Stripe itself. For Stripe, absorbing PayPal's massive user base and Venmo's consumer brand could accelerate its push beyond pure infrastructure into broader financial services — though integrating a public company of PayPal's scale would present significant regulatory and operational challenges.

articles.headline: Ukrainian Drone Strike Hits Russian Fertilizer Plant, Killing Seven
articles.subheadline: Acron Group's nitrogen facility in Smolensk region damaged in overnight attack, escalating risks to global fertilizer supply
articles.body:
A Ukrainian drone attack struck a nitrogen fertilizer facility operated by Acron Group in Russia's Smolensk region overnight, killing seven plant employees, according to Russian authorities.

The strike against one of Russia's major fertilizer producers marks a significant escalation in Ukraine's campaign targeting industrial infrastructure deep inside Russian territory. Acron, headquartered in Veliky Novgorod, is one of the world's largest producers of mineral fertilizers, with operations spanning nitrogen and compound fertilizer production. The Smolensk region sits roughly 400 kilometers from the Ukrainian border, underscoring the growing reach of Kyiv's drone capabilities nearly three years into the full-scale conflict.

The attack on critical agricultural-chemical infrastructure carries implications beyond the battlefield. Russia is a dominant force in global fertilizer markets, and any sustained disruption to production capacity could reverberate through commodity prices at a time when food security remains a pressing concern for developing nations. Nitrogen-based fertilizers are essential inputs for crop yields worldwide, and supply constraints have previously driven sharp price spikes — most notably in the months following Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, when fertilizer costs surged to record levels.

Acron Group, controlled by Russian billionaire Viatcheslav Kantor, produces millions of tons of fertilizer annually and exports to dozens of countries. While Western sanctions have targeted many sectors of the Russian economy, fertilizer exports have largely been shielded from restrictions due to concerns about global food supply disruptions. The extent of damage to the Smolensk facility and the timeline for restoring production were not immediately clear.

Ukraine has steadily expanded its long-range drone strike program over the course of the war, hitting energy infrastructure, military installations, and industrial facilities across western and central Russia. The strategy aims to impose economic costs on Moscow and disrupt supply chains that support the Russian war effort. The seven fatalities at the Acron plant represent one of the deadlier single strikes on a Russian civilian industrial site in recent months.

articles.headline: Santander Targets €20 Billion Profit by 2028 After Webster Acquisition
articles.subheadline: Europe's largest bank by market cap raises financial ambitions as U.S. expansion reshapes its growth strategy
articles.body:
Banco Santander SA has set an ambitious new profit target of more than €20 billion ($23.6 billion) by 2028, elevating its financial goals on the heels of its recently announced acquisition of U.S. lender Webster Financial Corp.

The Spanish banking giant's upgraded guidance signals management's confidence that the Webster deal will meaningfully accelerate its earnings trajectory. Santander has been steadily expanding its presence in the United States, one of the world's most profitable banking markets, and the acquisition of Connecticut-based Webster Financial represents a significant deepening of that commitment. The deal gives Santander a larger footprint in the U.S. commercial and consumer banking landscape, complementing its existing operations through Santander Consumer USA and other subsidiaries.

The €20 billion profit pledge would mark a substantial leap for the bank, which has already been on a strong upward trajectory under Chief Executive Ana Botín's leadership. Santander has benefited in recent years from higher interest rates across its key markets in Europe and Latin America, and the bank has worked to streamline operations through its digital transformation program. Adding Webster's balance sheet and customer base to that foundation gives analysts a clearer line of sight to the upgraded target.
